Title: The functions of steryl glycosides come to those who wait: Recent advances in plants, fungi, bacteria and animals

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt80 subfamily
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of UGT80B1 strongly reduces steryl glycosides/acyl steryl glycosides and causes defective seed development, transparent testa, increased seed coat permeability, and altered lipid polyester deposition in Arabidopsis
Common_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Steryl glycoside biosynthesis linked to lipid polyester precursor trafficking/export during seed coat development
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT80B1 is one of the two Arabidopsis sterol glucosyltransferases responsible for phytosteryl β-glycoside biosynthesis. 2. ugt80b1 mutants show stronger seed phenotypes than ugt80a2, including transparent testa and permeable seed coat. 3. Data support a role for steryl glycosides/acyl steryl glycosides in export or trafficking of extracellular lipid polyester precursors rather than an essential role in cellulose biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane lipid function; supports normal seed coat formation and deposition/export of lipid polyester-related materials
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Distinct from UGT80A2 by promoter-GUS studies; associated with seed developmental functions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Developing seeds/seed coat-relevant expression inferred from phenotype and promoter studies
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA insertion mutant analysis; double-mutant analysis; lipid profiling; microscopy; promoter-GUS analysis; rescue/complementation
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review summarizes primary study by DeBolt et al. Residual SG in double mutant suggests additional minor SG-producing activities.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Seed defects and reduced root growth when SG biosynthesis is impaired
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating UGT80B1 would strongly affect seed coat integrity and thus is not a straightforward improvement target; useful for studying extracellular lipid deposition mechanisms

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt80 subfamily
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Contributes to Arabidopsis steryl glycoside/acyl steryl glycoside biosynthesis; single mutant has milder phenotype but double mutant with ugt80b1 shows severe seed defects
Common_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Steryl glycoside biosynthesis in plant membranes and seed development-associated lipid trafficking
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. UGT80A2 is a functional Arabidopsis sterol β-glucosyltransferase. 2. Mutation reduces SG/ASG but causes weaker phenotypes than UGT80B1 loss. 3. Together with UGT80B1 it is required for normal seed morphology and seed coat lipid polyester deposition.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane lipid implicated in seed coat development and lipid precursor transport/export
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Distinct from UGT80B1 by promoter-GUS studies
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Broad tissue contribution inferred; weaker seed-specific impact than UGT80B1
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression; T-DNA insertion mutant analysis; double-mutant analysis; lipid profiling; promoter-GUS analysis
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review indicates overlapping but non-identical function with UGT80B1.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced SG biosynthesis can impair seed traits when combined with UGT80B1 deficiency
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful mechanistic target for dissecting membrane lipid roles, but reduction may compromise seed quality

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes lyso-phospholipid formation and increased acyl steryl glycoside proportion in Helicobacter pylori, contributing to membrane remodeling linked to pathogenicity
Common_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Membrane phospholipid remodeling and likely steryl glycoside acylation support
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Hp0499 activity correlates with increased lyso-phosphoglycerolipids and higher cholesteryl 6'-O-acyl-α-glucoside levels. 2. The protein may release fatty acids for acyl steryl glycoside synthesis or may itself act in acyltransferase-related remodeling. 3. Its activity is associated with colony phase variation and virulence-related membrane destabilization/remodeling.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports bacterial membrane remodeling and may help balance toxin release/pathogenicity with membrane stability
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with HpS phase/high phospholipase A2 activity states
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutational/phase variation analyses; lipid composition profiling
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function as direct SG acyltransferase versus phospholipase supplying fatty acid substrate remains unresolved in this review.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane destabilization due to lyso-phospholipid accumulation may threaten bacterial viability while enhancing virulence factor release
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antimicrobial target to disrupt Helicobacter membrane remodeling and host adaptation
Common_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Phase variation via DNA slippage in C-homopolymer tract; reversible frameshift regulation reported

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Proposed to use steryl glycosides as primers or glucosyl acceptors for steryl celloside formation during cellulose biosynthesis in cotton fiber
Common_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cellulose biosynthesis initiation/primer formation from steryl glycosides
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. In cotton fiber systems, steryl glycoside and steryl celloside formation correlated with cellulose biosynthesis. 2. Expression of cotton CesA-1 in yeast enabled in vitro synthesis of steryl cellosides from SG and UDP-glucose. 3. Arabidopsis SGT mutants did not support an essential universal role of SG in cellulose biosynthesis, so this function may be species- or tissue-specific.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Plasma membrane/cotton fiber cell wall interface
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Proposed primer function for cellulose synthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Cotton fiber; yeast membranes in heterologous assays
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Cell-free membrane assay; heterologous expression in yeast; in vitro steryl celloside synthesis assays
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review treats CesA as a candidate in SG metabolism context, but evidence remains indirect and Arabidopsis mutant data did not confirm a general requirement.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potentially relevant for fiber cellulose engineering if SG-primer model is validated in cotton

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt51 subfamily; contains PH-G domain
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to ergosterol to form ergosteryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for fungal steryl glycoside formation in Pichia pastoris
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential for ergosteryl β-glucoside biosynthesis and for micropexophagy of large methanol-induced peroxisomes in Pichia pastoris
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ugt51B1 is the single sterol glucosyltransferase responsible for SG biosynthesis in Pichia pastoris. 2. Deletion abolishes SG but cells remain viable. 3. Loss blocks micropexophagy, linking ergosteryl β-glucoside production to proper MIPA maturation and peroxisome degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Required for proper degradation of peroxisomes during micropexophagy
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: SG synthesized under glucose and methanol-related conditions; function critical during carbon-source shift-induced micropexophagy
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Induced/required during methanol-to-glucose switch for micropexophagy
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Preautophagosomal structure/MIPA precursor-associated; cytoplasmic peripheral membrane association
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion; complementation with GFP-tagged variants; lipid analysis; fluorescence localization; alcohol oxidase degradation assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Both catalytic activity and PH-G-mediated localization are required. Not essential for viability.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion causes autophagy-specific defect without obvious growth defect under standard conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal/bioprocess target for controlling pexophagy-related physiology
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Carbon-source shift from methanol to glucose

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt51 subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to ergosterol to form ergosteryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Confers low-level steryl glycoside biosynthetic capability in Saccharomyces cerevisiae; review notes no demonstrated essential autophagy role in this species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The S. cerevisiae gene YLR189C/Atg26 was identified as an ergosterol-β-glucosyltransferase by heterologous expression. 2. S. cerevisiae has low SG abundance and low SGT activity. 3. Unlike Pichia pastoris, Ugt51 is not required for autophagic processes investigated so far in S. cerevisiae.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Membrane steryl glycoside; specific physiological role in S. cerevisiae remains unclear
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Baker's yeast; E. coli heterologous assays
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional expression in E. coli; in vitro activity assay
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core review gene because it is a summarized functionally identified SGT, but strong metabolite-linked phenotype is not established in this species.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful comparative model for SGT evolution/function

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Bacterial glycosyltransferase family GT4; cholesterol α-glucosyltransferase
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to cholesterol to form cholesteryl α-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms the target product and precursor for acylated/phosphatidylated derivatives
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for cholesteryl α-glucoside formation and host immune escape
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for cholesteryl α-glucoside biosynthesis in Helicobacter pylori; its loss increases phagocytosis, T-cell activation, inflammatory response, and abolishes effective stomach colonization
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HP0421 glucosylates host-derived cholesterol to form cholesteryl α-glucoside. 2. Mutants defective in HP0421 cannot modulate host immune response efficiently and are strongly phagocytosed. 3. α-glucosylation, but not β-glucosylation, protects H. pylori from macrophage uptake and supports persistent colonization.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Immune evasion; protection from phagocytosis; supports host colonization
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Active when cholesterol is available from medium or host cells
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Probably inner membrane cytoplasmic face for synthesis; product enriched in outer membrane
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene mutation/knockout; lipid feeding; in vitro enzyme assays; phagocytosis assays; T-cell activation assays; mouse colonization studies
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Belongs to GT4 and retains anomeric configuration, unlike eukaryotic GT1 SGTs.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss impairs colonization fitness; cholesterol-rich cells without α-glucosylation become more visible to host immunity
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong antimicrobial target candidate to sensitize H. pylori to host immunity

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt51 subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to fungal sterols to form steryl β-glucosides
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control step for pathogenicity-linked SG pool
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for proper peroxisome degradation in appressoria and for invasion of cucumber host tissue
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Deletion of UGT51 reduced SGT activity in C. orbiculare, likely because additional Ugt54 enzymes remain. 2. Mutant appressoria generated mechanical penetration force but failed to invade normal host epidermis. 3. Defect is linked to impaired peroxisome degradation/autophagy in appressoria.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports host invasion via proper appressorium maturation and autophagic processes
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Relevant during appressorium development and host invasion
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Proper intracellular localization depends on N-terminal non-catalytic domains
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion; pathogenicity assays; appressorium function assays; truncated-gene expression studies; peroxisome degradation analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Residual activity indicates partial redundancy with Ugt54-type enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces pathogenicity on host tissue
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential antifungal target for controlling cucumber anthracnose

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt51 subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to fungal sterol to form steryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Functional SGT that restores SG biosynthesis when expressed in an SG-deficient Pichia pastoris mutant
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ugt51G3 from Fusarium graminearum was cloned as a full-length cDNA. 2. Expression in Pichia pastoris Δugt51 restored SG biosynthesis. 3. Confirms that Fusarium Ugt51G3 is a bona fide sterol β-glucosyltransferase.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous expression in Pichia pastoris mutant; thin-layer chromatography of lipids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review includes this as newly identified functional SGT.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for comparative engineering of fungal steryl glycoside pathways

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt54 subfamily; C-terminal MRS6 domain-containing
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to fungal sterol to form steryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Restores SG biosynthesis in SG-deficient Pichia pastoris, demonstrating functional SGT activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ugt54E7 from Fusarium graminearum is a functional sterol β-glucosyltransferase. 2. Heterologous expression rescued SG biosynthesis in Pichia pastoris Δugt51. 3. Supports the conclusion that Ugt54 proteins can act as fungal SGTs.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA cloning; heterologous expression in Pichia pastoris mutant; thin-layer chromatography
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Ugt54 proteins have distinctive C-terminal domain architecture compared with Ugt51/Ugt80.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for understanding fungal SGT isozyme diversity

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt54 subfamily; C-terminal MRS6 domain-containing
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to fungal sterol to form steryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Restores SG biosynthesis in SG-deficient Pichia pastoris, confirming functional sterol β-glucosyltransferase activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Ugt54F2 from Fusarium graminearum functions as a sterol β-glucosyltransferase. 2. Expression in Pichia pastoris Δugt51 restored SG formation. 3. Along with Ugt51G3 and Ugt54E7, it demonstrates multiple Fusarium SGT isozymes.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genomic DNA/cDNA expression; heterologous complementation in Pichia pastoris; TLC lipid analysis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Product in table listed as steryl β-Glcp.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful comparative enzyme for fungal SG pathway engineering

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Sterol glycosyltransferase; UGT superfamily GT1; Ugt53 subfamily
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers glucose from UDP-glucose to fungal sterol to form steryl β-glucoside
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Identified as functional sterol β-glucosyltransferase in Ustilago maydis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The review states that Ugt53A3 from Ustilago maydis was identified as a sterol β-glucosyltransferase by expression in E. coli and deletion analysis. 2. This expands functional SGT assignment beyond Ugt51/Ugt80. 3. Supports diversification of fungal SGT subfamilies.
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Ustilago maydis; E. coli heterologous system
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression in E. coli; generation of deletion mutant
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review text mentions Ugt53A3, while Table 2 lists Ugt53A1; nomenclature inconsistency noted.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for evolutionary study of sterol glycosyltransferases
